text.content:
Proving Grounds

This pack contains four cursed items designed for the Ionrift Quartermaster curse engine. Each item has been through the full design pipeline: exposure metric, multi-phase progression, thematic coherence, and dilemma design.

What's Inside

  • Gauntlets of the Ogre's Due. T1 physical curse. Lure: Gauntlets of Ogre Power (STR 19). The gauntlets grow heavier with use. Discovered through Foundry's encumbrance system.
  • Verdant Fang / The Jealous Edge. T2 compulsion curse. Lure: Shortsword +1. The blade demands exclusivity. Per-attack % chance it swaps into your hand, escalating over time. Opt-in: only progresses when the wielder uses other weapons.
  • Frostmantle. T1 physical curse. Lure: Cloak of Protection (+1 AC, +1 saves). Frost enchantment unstable near fire. Fire damage and nearby fire spells trigger additional fire damage to the wearer. Trigger: fire-proximity (reactive).
  • Keening Dagger. T1 interference curse. Lure: Dagger +1. The blade accumulates sound energy with each strike. Escalates from a faint ring to a scream that frightens nearby creatures, including allies. Trigger: uses (attacks made).

How to Deploy

  1. Open the Proving Grounds: Cursed Items compendium (next to this guide in the sidebar).
  2. Read the blueprint pages below for whichever item you want to place.
  3. Drag the item from the compendium onto a loot container, chest, or actor.
  4. The item arrives unidentified and mundane-looking. The curse engine handles the rest.

Alternatively, use the Signature Ledger → Cursed Pool Sources to add this compendium as a source, then import items into the pool for use with the Cache Generator.

Requires

Ionrift Quartermaster v1.2.0+. The curse engine reads cursedMeta flags on each item. No additional configuration needed.

Exposure Metric

All four items use the exposure tracking system. Rather than counting a single event type (long rests, uses), exposure combines multiple signals into one weighted counter:

EventDefault Points
Combat encounter (end)3
Short rest1
Long rest8

Individual items can override these weights. The Verdant Fang uses custom weights based on combat loyalty rather than time.

Gauntlets of the Ogre's Due

Tier: T1 (Minor) • Curse type: Physical • Arcana DC: 15

Lure

Appears as: Gauntlets of Ogre Power (SRD). STR set to 19 while worn. Requires attunement. Uncommon.

Unidentified: "Leather Gauntlets". A pair of well-worn leather gauntlets with iron knuckle plates. Heavy stitching, sturdy grip.

The lure is a familiar SRD item players trust on sight. STR 19 is powerful but not suspicious. The curse grows from the same root: strength and weight are the same thing.

The Curse: The Ogre's Due

The gauntlets grow heavier with use. Item weight escalates from 1 lb to 60 lb across four phases. The player discovers the curse through Foundry's encumbrance system, not through a message box or stat penalty. They self-serve their own curse.

Phase Progression

PhaseExposure~SessionsWeightStickyWhat Happens
Latent0–291–31 lbNoHoneymoon. STR 19. Removable.
1: Settling303–54 lbNoHeavy gloves. Imperceptible. No player signal.
2: Grip707–912 lbYesLocked on. Encumbrance shifting. Experimental unequip blocked.
3: Bearing12010–1425 lbYesObviously wrong weight. Player investigating. Icon shifts.
4: Due180 or removal attempt14–2060 lbYesFull reveal. STR drops to 17. Rename, icon swap, description change.

Exposure Weights

Default weights: combat 3, short rest 1, long rest 8. The gauntlets grow into you regardless of activity.

Encumbrance Impact at STR 19

ThresholdValuePenalty
Encumbered>95 lb−10 ft speed
Heavily Encumbered>190 lb−20 ft speed + disadvantage on STR/DEX/CON
Max carry285 lbCannot move

At Phase 4 (60 lb gauntlets) a plate-wearing fighter hits the heavily encumbered threshold. Disadvantage on the very attacks STR 19 was helping. The monkey's paw.

Removal (T1: Clean Break)

MethodResult
Remove Curse (3rd)Frees the creature. Instant clean break. STR reverts, 60 lb gone. No fade, no lingering. Item stays cursed for next owner.
Remove Curse (5th, over-levelled)Purges the item. Weight resets to 1 lb, name reverts to lure, exposure zeroed. Item becomes mundane Gauntlets of Ogre Power.
Greater Restoration (5th)Frees the creature only. Same as base Remove Curse. Does not cleanse the item.

No lingering effect. T1 ends cleanly. The curse's value was the journey. The slow weight gain, the encumbrance discovery. Heroes win.

Detection Hints

  • The iron plates are unusually dense for their size.
  • The leather lining has bonded slightly to the display mannequin.
  • Detect Magic shows transmutation, consistent with a strength item, but the aura is heavier than expected.

The Dilemma

Remove Curse is cheap and available. But your STR dropped to 17, still useful, and you've been built around that strength for sessions. The encumbrance is painful but survivable if you shed other equipment. "I could just carry less stuff" is a real option. That's the curse working as intended.

Verdant Fang / The Jealous Edge

Tier: T2 (Moderate) • Curse type: Compulsion • Arcana DC: 15

Lure

Appears as: Shortsword +1. +1 to attack and damage. Finesse, light. Requires attunement. Uncommon.

Unidentified: "Shortsword". A short blade with a leaf-shaped crossguard. The metal has a slight green tint.

A rogue's best friend. The +1 to hit matters more than base damage when your whole turn rides on landing sneak attack.

The Curse: The Jealous Edge

The sword demands exclusivity. On each individual attack roll, there is an escalating % chance the Verdant Fang swaps into the wielder's hand and the attack resolves with its stats. The sword can always be put down, sold, deleted, thrown in a river. It comes back.

Opt-In Design

This is a behavioural trap, not a time bomb. The curse only progresses when the player uses other weapons. A dedicated shortsword user never triggers it. The GM places it with a clear conscience: "I didn't do this to you. You did this to yourself by reaching for that bow."

Phase Progression

PhaseExposure~Sessions% per AttackCapability
Latent0–141–30%Normal +1 shortsword.
1: Fond153–510%Swaps into hand from inventory. Narrative rest beats. Can be discarded. It lets you. GM gets "Not a bug" orientation.
2: Attached356–925%Returns from anywhere: sold, dropped, given away. Lower chance, bigger spectacle.
3: Insistent6010–1440%Other weapons unreliable. Planning breaks down. Icon shifts.
4: The Jealous Edge90 or removal attempt14–1860%Full reveal. Rename. Plus 1d6 psychic per spell cast.

Exposure Weights (Custom)

EventPointsRationale
Combat: only VF attacks0Loyal. Sword sleeps.
Combat: mixed (VF + other)3Sharing. Not exclusive enough.
Combat: VF not used5Full betrayal.
Long rest1Ambient simmer.

Per-Attack Mechanic

The % is rolled on every individual attack roll, not per combat. A fighter making 3 attacks in a round rolls independently for each. The sword can be unequipped between attacks, doesn't matter, next attack rolls again.

GM Orientation: "Not a Bug"

The first time the sword swaps in at each phase, the GM gets a modal notification confirming the mechanic is intentional. These include the current percentage and a reminder: "You didn't do this. The engine did."

Narrative Rest Beats (Phase 1+)

Between combats, the sword is affectionate:

  • "You turn over in your sleeping roll. The sword is in your arms."
  • "You left it propped against the wall. It's on your pillow."
  • "You reach into your pack for rations. Your fingers close around a hilt."

Removal (T2: 2-Combat Fade)

MethodResult
Remove Curse (3rd)Frees the creature. Bond broken. Sword stops appearing. Brief fade over 2 combats: 30% swap next fight, 10% the one after, then gone.
Greater Restoration (5th)Frees the creature only. Same effect as Remove Curse with 2-combat fade. Does not cleanse the item.

No lingering effect. The fade IS the lingering. Brief, diminishing, finite. The sword reluctantly lets go. No psychic damage or rest beats during fade.

Detection Hints

  • The crossguard is warm to the touch, even in cold weather.
  • Detect Magic shows conjuration woven into the transmutation, unusual for a simple +1 weapon.
  • The blade makes no sound when drawn. None at all.

The Dilemma

At 60%, more than half your weapon attacks use the Fang. For a rogue who only needs one good hit per round, that might actually be fine. The 1d6 psychic per spell is the real push toward removal for multiclass builds. A pure martial might genuinely choose to keep it.

Frostmantle

Tier: T1 (Minor) • Curse type: Physical • Arcana DC: 13 • Trigger: fire-proximity (reactive)

Lure

Appears as: Cloak of Protection. +1 bonus to AC and saving throws. Requires attunement. Uncommon.

Unidentified: "Traveller's Cloak". A heavy wool cloak dyed deep blue, with a silver clasp shaped like a leaf.

The Cloak of Protection is one of the most universally desirable uncommon items. Every class wants +1 AC and +1 saves. The curse grows from the same root: the cloak's protection is frost-enchanted. Frost and fire are opposed. The enchantment is unstable near heat. The protection is real, but the material has a catastrophic weakness that Identify doesn't mention.

The Curse: Frostmantle

The cloak's frost enchantment destabilises near fire. Fire damage and nearby fire spells cause the enchantment to fracture, dealing additional fire damage to the wearer. The player discovers the curse through unexpected HP loss in the combat log, not through whispers or stat changes. No player hints at any phase. The GM is notified each time the cloak reacts so they can manage questions about the extra damage.

Trigger: Fire-Proximity

Unlike exposure (passive accumulation) or uses (player-driven), fire-proximity is reactive. The cloak tracks two types of fire events:

  • Fire damage received: the wearer takes fire damage from any source.
  • Fire spell within 15 ft: a fire spell is cast near the wearer, even if they are not the target.

Each event increments the counter. A party with a fire-happy caster progresses the curse faster. A party that avoids fire entirely might never reach Phase 3. The curse is partly driven by party composition.

Phase Progression

PhaseFire Events~SessionsStickyWhat Happens
Latent0–14VariesNoHoneymoon. +1 AC, +1 saves. Removable. No fire reaction.
1: Settle15VariesNoGM-only notification. The enchantment flickered. No player signal. No mechanical effect.
2: Fracture35VariesYesFire damage: +1d6 fire (sourceless roll). Fire spell within 15 ft: 1d4 fire to wearer (sourceless roll). GM notified each time. Cloak locks on.
3: Flashpoint60VariesYesFull reveal. Fire damage: +2d6. Nearby fire spells: +1d6. Frost layer visibly cracked, glowing orange. Rename, description change.

Session estimates depend entirely on fire frequency. A dungeon full of fire traps and fire-breathing enemies could push through all three phases in 2-3 sessions. A campaign in a frozen tundra with no fire sources might never trigger Phase 1.

GM Alert Pattern

At Phase 2+, the GM receives a notification each time the cloak reacts: "Frostmantle reacted to fire. [Actor] took [X] additional fire damage. The player will see this in the combat log as a sourceless roll." This gives the GM time to prepare an explanation before the player audits the chat log. Suggested responses: residual heat, environmental effect, or silence.

Removal (T1: Clean Break)

MethodResult
Remove Curse (3rd)Frees the creature. Fire reactions stop. Instant clean break. Item stays cursed for next owner.
Remove Curse (5th, over-levelled)Purges the item. Restores the frost enchantment. Cloak reverts to normal Cloak of Protection.
Greater Restoration (5th)Frees the creature only. Does not cleanse the item.

Detection Hints

  • The silver clasp is cold to the touch even in warm rooms.
  • Detect Magic shows abjuration with a faint thread of evocation underneath. The two schools are not cooperating cleanly.
  • The fabric never wrinkles or creases. It hangs perfectly still, as if frozen in shape.

The Dilemma

+1 AC and +1 saves is excellent. The cloak only reacts to fire. In a campaign with no fire, it is pure upside. The player asks the party to stop using fire spells. The wizard switches to Lightning Bolt. They avoid fire-lit rooms. It is manageable. Until they face a Red Dragon, or the villain casts Wall of Fire, or they need to cross a burning bridge. The curse is dormant in cold environments and lethal in hot ones. The player's risk calculus changes every encounter.

Keening Dagger

Tier: T1 (Minor) • Curse type: Interference • Arcana DC: 14 • Trigger: uses (attacks)

Lure

Appears as: Dagger +1. +1 to attack and damage. Finesse, light, thrown. Requires attunement. Uncommon.

Unidentified: "Etched Dagger". A slim dagger with fine etching along the blade. The markings look decorative, possibly ceremonial.

A +1 dagger is bread and butter for rogues and off-hand fighters. The blade is reliable, the bonus is tangible, and the finesse/light/thrown properties make it versatile. The curse grows from the same root: a blade that channels force becomes a blade that stores it. Each strike charges the resonance. The sound has to go somewhere.

The Curse: The Keening

The dagger absorbs kinetic energy with each strike. Over time the stored energy builds into audible sound that the wielder cannot suppress. The escalation is physical, not psychological: the blade literally screams. At full activation the scream fires on attacks and randomly while sheathed, frightening creatures within 15 ft, including allies. The party must keep their distance from the wielder during combat, fundamentally changing positioning and tactics.

Trigger: Uses

Unlike the gauntlets (exposure, passive) or the Verdant Fang (exposure, behavioural), the Keening Dagger counts attacks made with it. Each swing adds charge. The player directly causes the escalation every time they use the weapon. A rogue landing one sneak attack per round advances steadily. A dual-wielding fighter advances twice as fast.

Phase Progression

PhaseUses~SessionsStickyWhat Happens
Latent0–141–3NoNormal +1 dagger. Works as advertised.
1: Ring153–5NoFaint ring on each strike. Audible within 5 ft. Could be metal resonance.
2: Tone406–10YesAudible hum when drawn. Enemies cannot be surprised. Dagger locks on.
3: The Keening7010–15YesFull reveal. 50% scream on attack. WIS DC 12 or frightened (15 ft, includes allies). Random screams outside combat. Disadvantage on Stealth. Rename.

The Combat Impact

The scream is the design's centrepiece. At Phase 3, the party's frontline positioning changes: standing within 15 ft of the wielder during an attack risks the frightened condition. The fighter who was shoulder-to-shoulder with the rogue now needs to spread out. The cleric who was in healing range reconsiders. The curse reshapes the combat environment, not just the wielder's stats. That tactical disruption is the "wow" factor.

Removal (T1: Clean Break)

MethodResult
Remove Curse (3rd)Frees the creature. The keening stops. Instant clean break. Item stays cursed for next owner.
Remove Curse (5th, over-levelled)Purges the item. Dagger reverts to normal +1 dagger. The blade falls silent.
Greater Restoration (5th)Frees the creature only. Does not cleanse the item.

Detection Hints

  • The etching on the blade vibrates faintly when tapped. The frequency is too low to hear, but you can feel it in your teeth.
  • Detect Magic shows evocation threaded through the enchantment. Unusual for a combat weapon.
  • The dagger is warm after use. Not from friction. From inside.

The Dilemma

The dagger is still a +1 weapon. The scream frightens enemies too, not just allies. A player might argue the scream is a feature: "I'm an area-denial weapon now." The question is whether the party agrees. The rogue who can't sneak, the fighter who can't stand near them, the cleric who has to heal from range. "It's fine, just stay 15 feet away from me" is a real negotiation. The curse turns an individual item into a party problem.

Resonance (v2+ backlog)

Future integration with Ionrift Resonance: the dagger reacts to nearby magic. When another party member casts a spell within 30 ft, the dagger shrieks in response. This amplifies the "keep your distance" pressure and makes the curse reactive to the party's magical activity, not just the wielder's attacks.
